Q1: I tried to keep a structured routine, dedicating about four hours daily to studying across my 17 online courses. I'd usually start after dinner since that's when the house is quieter and I could focus better. I made weekly schedules in my notebook, blocking out time for reading materials and taking notes. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when to use internet for downloading content versus when to study offline.
Q2: My pattern definitely shifted based on deadlines and how anxious I was feeling about upcoming exams. I aimed for consistency, but honestly, when multiple assignments piled up, I'd have to abandon my routine and just focus on whatever was most urgent. Some weeks I'd study steadily every day, other weeks it was chaotic bursts of activity. The anxiety made it hard to stick to plans sometimes.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew new materials were posted or when I needed to check assignment deadlines. Sometimes the anxiety would push me to log in just to make sure I hadn't missed anything important. I also logged in frequently to download videos and readings so I could study them offline later when my internet was spotty. Peer support group reminders also prompted me to check in and see what others were discussing.
Q4: Yes, checking the LMS became almost automatic, especially in the mornings before my other responsibilities. I'd log in with my tea, scroll through announcements, and mentally plan my day around what needed attention. It took a few weeks to build this habit, but once I connected with a few peers through the discussion forums, I had more reason to return regularly. The routine helped manage my anxiety about missing things.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful because I could watch them multiple times to really understand concepts. I'd watch them once online, then download them to rewatch offline while taking detailed notes. The reading materials in PDF format were also great since I could print them at the library when I had some money. Discussion posts helped too because seeing how peers understood things gave me different perspectives.
Q6: I often skipped the optional supplementary readings because I simply didn't have time with 17 courses to manage. Some of the interactive activities required stable internet which I couldn't always guarantee, so I'd delay those indefinitely. I also minimized my time on discussion forums beyond the required posts because typing out responses took time I needed for actual studying. It wasn't that I didn't value them, just survival mode.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or helped me understand a concept I was struggling with. If I could see immediate relevance to my grades or assignments, I'd prioritize it. Peer discussions that clarified confusing topics also felt valuable. Anything that felt like busy work or repetition of things I already understood, I'd skip when time was tight.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. Required ones created this pressure and anxiety, but at least I knew they mattered for my grade. Optional ones made me feel guilty when I couldn't do them, like I was missing opportunities to learn more. Sometimes the optional content was actually more interesting, but I had to be practical about where my energy went given my workload and stress levels.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and videos when I had good internet access, then study from those for hours without being online. I filled notebooks with handwritten notes and summaries because writing helps me remember better. I also borrowed textbooks from classmates and studied from those. My LMS activity probably shows way less than the actual time I spent learning.
Q10: Many evenings I'd sit with my printed notes and downloaded materials, reviewing and rewriting key concepts for hours. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. When I studied with my peer support group in person, we'd work through problems together using our notes, but the LMS wouldn't capture that collaboration. I also did a lot of mental review while commuting or doing chores, just going over concepts in my head.
Q11: Yes, this was my main strategy honestly. I'd access a video lecture once, download it, and then watch it three or four more times offline over the following days. Same with readings - I'd download them, maybe print them if I could afford to, and study from the physical copies. My one-time access in the LMS really doesn't reflect how much I engaged with those materials afterward.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when assignments from multiple courses overlapped. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed, unable to decide what to tackle first. Financial stress at home also affected my concentration - it's hard to focus on coursework when you're worried about basic things. During those times, I'd still log in but feel disconnected from the actual learning.
Q13: The workload across 17 courses was genuinely overwhelming sometimes, and it definitely affected how I used the LMS. When I was exhausted, I'd log in but just skim through things without really absorbing them. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules temporarily because facing them increased my anxiety. Family responsibilities also meant some weeks I had less time for the LMS than others.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd watch a video but my mind was elsewhere, worried about other deadlines or personal issues. I'd technically complete the activity but retain almost nothing. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was keeping up. The high anxiety made it hard to be mentally present even when I was physically logged in and clicking through content.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting them feel overwhelming, so I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more prepared. Some assignments required resources or software I didn't have easy access to, so I'd delay hoping to find a solution. My completion rate suffered because of this avoidance pattern, even though I knew it was hurting my grades.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd become intensely active in the days before something was due, logging in multiple times to check requirements and submit work. Without a deadline, I might let materials sit for a while before engaging with them. The anxiety around deadlines was a double-edged sword - it motivated me to act but also made the process stressful and sometimes led to rushed, lower-quality work.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced my engagement, though I didn't always get it quickly. When I received positive feedback, it boosted my motivation and made me more likely to engage with similar activities. Critical feedback sometimes increased my anxiety initially, but I'd eventually use it to improve. The peer support helped me process feedback better - talking through comments with others made them feel less personal and more constructive.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules helped me organize my approach, but having 17 courses meant I couldn't give each one the attention it deserved. Courses with more flexible deadlines were easier to manage around my other responsibilities. The ones with rigid weekly requirements added to my stress because falling behind felt catastrophic. I appreciated when instructors provided clear roadmaps of what to expect.
Q19: Courses that offered downloadable content made engagement much easier given my inconsistent internet access. Clear, concise instructions reduced my anxiety about whether I was doing things correctly. However, courses that required constant online presence or real-time participation were harder for me. Discussion requirements felt manageable, but too many mandatory interactions became overwhelming alongside everything else I was juggling.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying but struggling to produce quality work. They might be reading, watching, researching, but anxiety or confusion prevents them from completing assignments. Or maybe they're perfectionists who keep revising and never feel ready to submit. It could also be that life circumstances are making it hard to finish things even though they're putting in effort to learn.
Q21: That definitely resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was decent even though my assignment completion wasn't great, partly because I studied extensively offline. Some students might be efficient learners who don't need to spend hours on the LMS to understand material. Or they might have strong backgrounds that let them grasp concepts quickly. The LMS activity isn't the full picture of someone's learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone is active, how often they log in, and what they click on. But they miss the quality of engagement - whether someone is actually thinking deeply or just going through motions. They can't capture offline studying, peer discussions outside the platform, or the mental effort someone puts into understanding. They also can't show why someone's activity drops - stress, illness, family issues.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks for grades. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts, and thinking about how things apply beyond the course. For me, engagement also involves connecting with peers and feeling like part of a learning community. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just time spent logged in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from downloaded and printed materials, or the hours spent with my peer support group working through problems together. It can't capture the anxiety I managed while trying to stay on track, or the resilience it took to keep going despite limited resources. My emotional journey through the course - the stress, the small victories, the growth - none of that appears in click data.
